metadata.document_title: Expedited Programs for Serious Conditions — Drugs and Biologics
metadata.regulatory_framework: FD&C Act sections 506(a)–506(c); 21 CFR part 312 subpart E; PDUFA
metadata.notes: Covers four expedited programs: Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, Accelerated Approval, Priority Review. Includes Appendix 1 (processes for Fast Track, Breakthrough Therapy, Priority Review designations) and Appendix 2 (Rolling Review). No prior extraction found in v2/v3/v4.

rules.rule_text: A serious condition is a disease or condition associated with morbidity that has substantial impact on day-to-day functioning. Short-lived and self-limiting morbidity will usually not be sufficient, but the morbidity need not be irreversible if it is persistent or recurrent. Whether a condition is serious is a matter of clinical judgment, based on its impact on such factors as survival, day-to-day functioning, or the likelihood that the disease, if left untreated, will progress from a less severe condition to a more serious one.
rules.source_section: Section III.A — Serious Condition
rules.verbatim_quote: A serious condition is a disease or condition associated with morbidity that has substantial impact on day-to-day functioning. Short-lived and self-limiting morbidity will usually not be sufficient, but the morbidity need not be irreversible if it is persistent or recurrent.
exceptions: Short-lived and self-limiting morbidity generally insufficient

rules.rule_text: Available therapy means a medical product or therapy approved for the same indication as the drug under investigation that is relevant to and routinely used in the current standard of care in the United States. When determining whether a drug provides a meaningful advantage over available therapy, FDA considers only available therapies as defined — approved for the same indication and relevant to current standard of care.
rules.source_section: Section III.B — Available Therapy
rules.verbatim_quote: Available therapy means a medical product or therapy approved for the same indication as the drug under investigation that is relevant to and routinely used in the current standard of care in the United States.
exceptions: Therapies approved only under accelerated approval may not constitute available therapy for unmet need determination

rules.rule_text: A drug fulfills an unmet medical need if it provides a therapy where none exists, or may be superior to available therapy in one or more of seven ways: (1) more effective treatment or prevention; (2) elimination or substantial reduction of a treatment-limiting adverse reaction; (3) documented enhancement of patient compliance that improves serious outcomes; (4) ability to treat patients unresponsive to available therapy; (5) to treat a patient population that cannot tolerate available therapy; (6) use in combination with available therapy to enhance effect; or (7) addresses an emerging or anticipated public health need.
rules.source_section: Section III.C — Unmet Medical Need
rules.verbatim_quote: A drug fulfills an unmet medical need if, among other things, it provides a therapy where none exists. Additionally, a drug may fulfill an unmet medical need if it may be superior to available therapy.
exceptions: Unmet need may exist even when available therapy exists only under accelerated approval

rules.rule_text: Fast track designation criteria require: (1) the drug is intended to treat a serious or life-threatening condition, AND (2) the drug demonstrates potential to address an unmet medical need for that condition, OR the drug is a qualified infectious disease product (QIDP). Both criteria must be met for fast track designation (unless QIDP).
rules.source_section: Section V.A — Qualifying Criteria for Fast Track Designation
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA will grant a fast track designation if: (1) the drug is intended for the treatment of a serious or life-threatening condition AND (2) the drug demonstrates the potential to address unmet medical need for the condition.
exceptions: QIDP designation provides alternative pathway under GAIN Act

rules.rule_text: FDA will respond to fast track designation requests within 60 calendar days of receipt of the request. This applies to both designation and nondesignation letters.
rules.source_section: Section V.B / Appendix 1.A.4 — FDA Response
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA will respond to fast track designation requests within 60 calendar days of receipt of the request.

rules.rule_text: Fast track designation features include: (1) more frequent scheduled meetings and communications to discuss the drug's development plan and ensure collection of appropriate data needed to support drug approval; (2) more frequent written correspondence about such things as trial design, use of biomarkers, and the extent of data needed to support approval; and (3) eligibility for rolling review of portions of the NDA or BLA before the complete application is submitted.
rules.source_section: Section V.B — Features of Fast Track Designation
rules.verbatim_quote: Fast track designation is associated with: more frequent scheduled meetings and communications to discuss the drug's development plan and ensure collection of appropriate data needed to support drug approval; more frequent written correspondence about such things as trial design, use of biomarkers, and the extent of data needed to support approval.

rules.rule_text: Rolling review — a key feature of fast track and breakthrough therapy designations — allows FDA to review completed sections of the NDA or BLA before the sponsor submits the complete application. To use rolling review, sponsors must submit a rolling review request and schedule. FDA will evaluate the request and respond within 60 days. Sponsors must pay PDUFA user fees at the time of the first rolling review submission.
rules.source_section: Appendix 2 — Rolling Review Process
rules.verbatim_quote: Rolling review allows FDA to review completed sections of the NDA or BLA before the sponsor submits the complete application.
exceptions: PDUFA user fees must be paid at first rolling review submission

rules.rule_text: Breakthrough therapy designation criteria require: (1) the drug is intended to treat a serious or life-threatening condition AND (2) preliminary clinical evidence indicates the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over existing therapies on one or more clinically significant endpoints. Nonclinical data alone are not sufficient to support breakthrough therapy designation — preliminary clinical evidence is required.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A — Qualifying Criteria for Breakthrough Therapy Designation
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA will grant breakthrough therapy designation if: (1) the drug is intended for the treatment of a serious or life-threatening disease or condition AND (2) preliminary clinical evidence indicates that the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over existing therapies on 1 or more clinically significant endpoints.
exceptions: Nonclinical data alone insufficient; clinical evidence required

rules.rule_text: A clinically significant endpoint for breakthrough therapy designation purposes is generally one that measures an effect on irreversible morbidity or mortality (IMM) or on symptoms that represent serious consequences of the disease. An effect on a pharmacodynamic biomarker will not generally be considered a clinically significant endpoint unless the biomarker reasonably predicts clinical benefit.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.2 — Clinically Significant Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: A clinically significant endpoint generally refers to an endpoint that measures an effect on irreversible morbidity or mortality (IMM) or on symptoms that represent serious consequences of the disease.
exceptions: Biomarker endpoints acceptable if reasonably predictive of clinical benefit

rules.rule_text: Substantial improvement for breakthrough therapy purposes means an effect that represents a meaningful difference — not merely a statistically significant difference — over existing therapies, suggesting a qualitative or large quantitative improvement. Preliminary clinical evidence must demonstrate that the drug 'may' demonstrate substantial improvement; certainty of benefit is not required at the time of designation request.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1 — Substantial Improvement
rules.verbatim_quote: preliminary clinical evidence indicates that the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over existing therapies on 1 or more clinically significant endpoints
exceptions: 'May demonstrate' standard — certainty not required at designation stage

rules.rule_text: FDA will respond to breakthrough therapy designation requests within 60 calendar days of receipt of the request.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1.B.4 — FDA Response
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA will respond to breakthrough therapy designation requests within 60 calendar days of receipt of the request.

rules.rule_text: Breakthrough therapy designation features include: (1) all features available to fast track designated drugs; (2) intensive guidance on an efficient drug development program, beginning as early as Phase 1; (3) organizational commitment involving senior managers and experienced staff from FDA; and (4) rolling review. The intensive guidance is intended to identify the most efficient development pathway.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B — Features of Breakthrough Therapy Designation
rules.verbatim_quote: All of the fast track program features are available to breakthrough therapy designated drugs. Additionally, FDA will provide: intensive guidance on an efficient drug development program, beginning as early as Phase 1; organizational commitment involving senior managers and experienced staff from FDA.

rules.rule_text: Breakthrough therapy designation requests should not be submitted until the sponsor has preliminary clinical evidence indicating that the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement on clinically significant endpoints. FDA expects that in most cases breakthrough therapy designation requests will be submitted as an amendment to the IND. Ideally, FDA should receive the request before initiation of the clinical trial(s) intended to serve as the primary basis for demonstration of efficacy.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1.B.1 — When to Send a Designation Submission
rules.verbatim_quote: sponsors should not send breakthrough therapy designation requests until they have preliminary clinical evidence indicating that '. . . the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over existing therapies on 1 or more clinically significant endpoints.' FDA therefore expects that in most cases breakthrough therapy designation requests would be submitted as an amendment to the IND.

rules.rule_text: Accelerated approval under FD&C Act 506(c) is available for drugs for serious or life-threatening conditions that provide a meaningful advantage over available therapy and have an effect on a surrogate endpoint or intermediate clinical endpoint that is reasonably likely to predict an effect on irreversible morbidity or mortality (IMM) or other clinical benefit. Drugs approved under accelerated approval are subject to required postmarketing confirmatory trials.
rules.source_section: Section VII.A — Overview of Accelerated Approval
rules.verbatim_quote: Accelerated approval is available for drugs for serious or life-threatening conditions that provide a meaningful advantage over available therapy based on an effect on a surrogate endpoint or intermediate clinical endpoint that is reasonably likely to predict an effect on IMM or other clinical benefit.

rules.rule_text: For accelerated approval, a drug must provide a meaningful advantage over available therapy. Meaningful advantage includes situations where the drug provides a therapy where none exists, or may be superior to available therapy in any of several ways, including effectiveness, safety profile, ability to treat nonresponders, ability to be used in patients intolerant of available therapy, or use as combination therapy. Meaningful advantage may exist even where there is no demonstrated direct efficacy or safety advantage.
rules.source_section: Section VII.A.2 — Meaningful Advantage Over Available Therapy
rules.verbatim_quote: which a drug could be shown to provide a meaningful advantage over available therapy, including some in which there may not be a demonstrated direct efficacy or safety advantage.

rules.rule_text: The two types of endpoints that can be used as a basis for accelerated approval are: (1) a surrogate endpoint that is considered reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit, and (2) an intermediate clinical endpoint that can be measured earlier than IMM and is reasonably likely to predict the drug's effect on IMM or other clinical benefit. These are referred to as surrogate endpoints and intermediate clinical endpoints, respectively.
rules.source_section: Section VII.B — Accelerated Approval Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: The two types of endpoints that can be used as a basis for accelerated approval are: (1) a surrogate endpoint that is considered reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit and (2) a clinical endpoint that can be measured earlier than IMM that is reasonably likely to predict an effect on IMM or other clinical benefit.

rules.rule_text: For accelerated approval purposes, a surrogate endpoint is a marker — such as a laboratory measurement, radiographic image, physical sign, or other measure — that is thought to predict clinical benefit but is not itself a measure of clinical benefit. The strength of evidence supporting the marker determines whether it is: (a) a validated surrogate endpoint supporting traditional approval; (b) a surrogate endpoint reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit supporting accelerated approval; or (c) a marker with insufficient evidence that cannot support either approval pathway.
rules.source_section: Section VII.B.1 — Surrogate Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: For purposes of accelerated approval, a surrogate endpoint is a marker, such as a laboratory measurement, radiographic image, physical sign, or other measure, that is thought to predict clinical benefit, but is not itself a measure of clinical benefit.

rules.rule_text: For accelerated approval purposes, an intermediate clinical endpoint is a measurement of a therapeutic effect that can be measured earlier than IMM and is considered reasonably likely to predict the drug's effect on IMM or other clinical benefit. Approvals based on clinical endpoints other than IMM will be considered under accelerated approval only when it is essential to determine effects on IMM or other clinical benefit in order to confirm the predicted clinical benefit.
rules.source_section: Section VII.B.2 — Intermediate Clinical Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: For purposes of accelerated approval, an intermediate clinical endpoint is a measurement of a therapeutic effect that can be measured earlier than an effect on IMM and is considered reasonably likely to predict the drug's effect on IMM or other clinical benefit.
exceptions: FDA will not grant accelerated approval to products that meet standards for traditional approval

rules.rule_text: FDA will not grant accelerated approval to products that meet standards for traditional approval. Sponsors considering a development program for accelerated approval based on an intermediate clinical endpoint should discuss their development program with the appropriate review division early in drug development.
rules.source_section: Section VII.B.2 — Intermediate Clinical Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA will not grant accelerated approval to products that meet standards for traditional approval.

rules.rule_text: Drugs granted accelerated approval must meet the same statutory standards for safety and effectiveness as those granted traditional approval. For effectiveness, the standard is substantial evidence based on adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations. For safety, the standard is having sufficient information to determine whether the drug is safe under conditions of the proposed labeling. FDA evaluates accelerated approval evidence to ensure doubts about the relationship of the surrogate effect to clinical benefit are resolved by postapproval studies.
rules.source_section: Section VII.C — Evidentiary Criteria for Accelerated Approval
rules.verbatim_quote: Drugs granted accelerated approval must meet the same statutory standards for safety and effectiveness as those granted traditional approval. For effectiveness, the standard is substantial evidence based on adequate and well-controlled clinical investigations. For safety, the standard is having sufficient information to determine whether the drug is safe for use under conditions prescribed, recommended, or suggested in the proposed labeling.

rules.rule_text: Determining whether an endpoint is reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit requires evidence of: (1) understanding of the disease process — specifically whether the surrogate is causally on the pathway of disease or correlates with the clinical outcome; and (2) understanding of the relationship between the drug's effect and the disease process. Pharmacologic activity alone is not sufficient. Clinical data must support that the relationship of the endpoint to clinical outcome is reasonably likely.
rules.source_section: Section VII.C — Evidentiary Criteria for Accelerated Approval
rules.verbatim_quote: The empirical evidence may include '. . . epidemiological, pathophysiological, therapeutic, pharmacologic, or other evidence developed using biomarkers, for example, or other scientific methods or tools.' Evidence of pharmacologic activity alone is not sufficient, however. Clinical data should be provided to support a conclusion that a relationship of an effect on the surrogate endpoint or intermediate clinical endpoint to an effect on the clinical outcome is reasonably likely.

rules.rule_text: Examples of surrogate endpoints FDA has used to support accelerated approval include: (1) prolonged suppression of HIV viral load in plasma (predicts morbidity/mortality in HIV disease); (2) clearance of bacteria from blood stream (predicts clinical resolution of infection); (3) 6-month follow-up sputum culture status and infection relapse rate (predicts resolution of pulmonary tuberculosis); (4) decrease in iron stores for thalassemia patients (predicts reduction in transfusion-related adverse events); and (5) radiographic evidence of tumor shrinkage — response rate — in certain cancer types (predicts improvement in overall survival).
rules.source_section: Section VII.B.1 — Examples of Surrogate Endpoints
rules.verbatim_quote: Examples of surrogate endpoints that FDA has used to support accelerated approval include the following: Prolonged suppression of HIV viral load in plasma has been shown to reduce the morbidity and mortality associated with HIV disease; Clearance of bacteria from the blood stream as evidenced by a laboratory measurement of bacteria in the blood has been considered reasonably likely to predict the clinical resolution of infection.

rules.rule_text: For accelerated approval surrogate endpoint evaluation, factors to consider include: (1) whether there is reliable and consistent epidemiologic evidence supporting the relationship between the endpoint and clinical benefit; (2) how precisely the epidemiologic relationship is defined (stronger correlation = stronger basis for accelerated approval); and (3) whether the effect on the surrogate endpoint has been shown to predict clinical benefit with another drug, particularly in the same or closely related pharmacological class.
rules.source_section: Section VII.C.2 — Understanding of the Relationship Between the Drug's Effect and the Disease Process
rules.verbatim_quote: Examples of factors to consider in identifying and assessing a surrogate endpoint thus include the following: Whether there is reliable and consistent epidemiologic evidence supporting the relationship between the endpoint and the intended clinical benefit; How precisely the epidemiologic relationship between the endpoint and clinical outcome is defined; Whether the effect on the surrogate endpoint has been shown to predict a clinical benefit with another drug or drugs.

rules.rule_text: Under accelerated approval, promotional materials must be submitted to FDA for consideration during the preapproval review period — copies of all promotional materials, including promotional labeling and advertisements, intended for dissemination or publication within 120 days following marketing approval. After 120 days following marketing approval, promotional materials must be submitted at least 30 days prior to the intended time of initial dissemination or publication.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.1 — Promotional Materials
rules.verbatim_quote: Unless otherwise informed by the Agency, an applicant must submit to the Agency for consideration during the preapproval review period copies of all promotional materials, including promotional labeling as well as advertisements, intended for dissemination or publication within 120 days following marketing approval. After 120 days following marketing approval, unless otherwise informed by the Agency, the applicant must submit promotional materials at least 30 days prior to the intended time of initial dissemination of the labeling or initial publication of the advertisement.

rules.rule_text: For drugs granted accelerated approval, postmarketing confirmatory trials must be conducted to verify and describe the anticipated effect on IMM or other clinical benefit. These trials must be completed with due diligence. The confirmatory trial protocol should be developed as early as possible, with stipulated timelines for enrollment and trial completion. There must be agreement between FDA and the sponsor on the design and conduct of the confirmatory trial(s).
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.2 — Confirmatory Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: For drugs granted accelerated approval, postmarketing confirmatory trials have been required to verify and describe the anticipated effect on IMM or other clinical benefit. These trials must be completed with due diligence.

rules.rule_text: If it is clear during development that a product is intended for accelerated approval on the basis of a surrogate or intermediate clinical endpoint, confirmatory trial(s) should be underway at the time the marketing application is submitted. If this is not clear until shortly before or after submission, there must be agreement on the design and conduct of such trial(s) before approval.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.2 — Confirmatory Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: If it is clear during development that a product is intended to be approved under accelerated approval on the basis of a surrogate endpoint or an intermediate clinical endpoint, confirmatory trial(s) should be underway at the time the marketing application is submitted. If it is not clear until shortly before or after submission of a marketing application that a surrogate endpoint or an intermediate clinical endpoint will be the proposed basis for accelerated approval, there should be agreement on the design and conduct of such trial(s) before approval.

rules.rule_text: FDA may withdraw accelerated approval of a drug or indication if any of the following occur: (1) a required trial fails to verify the predicted clinical benefit; (2) other evidence demonstrates the product is not safe or effective under the conditions of use; (3) the applicant fails to conduct any required postapproval trial with due diligence; or (4) the applicant disseminates false or misleading promotional materials.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.3 — Withdrawal of Accelerated Approval
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA may withdraw approval of a drug or indication approved under the accelerated approval pathway if, for example: A trial required to verify the predicted clinical benefit of the product fails to verify such benefit; Other evidence demonstrates that the product is not shown to be safe or effective under the conditions of use; The applicant fails to conduct any required postapproval trial of the drug with due diligence; The applicant disseminates false or misleading promotional materials relating to the product.

rules.rule_text: Withdrawal of accelerated approval uses the Notice of Opportunity for Hearing (NOOH) process. Upon receipt of NOOH, an applicant has 15 days to file a written request for a hearing. If the applicant does not request a hearing within 15 days, the applicant waives its opportunity for hearing. Alternatively, FDA may ask the applicant to voluntarily request withdrawal under 21 CFR 314.150(d).
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.3 — Withdrawal of Accelerated Approval
rules.verbatim_quote: If FDA determines there are grounds for withdrawal, the Agency may ask the applicant to request withdrawal of approval under § 314.150(d) or notify the applicant of FDA's proposal to withdraw approval in a notice of opportunity for hearing (NOOH). Upon receipt of an NOOH, an applicant has 15 days to file a written request for a hearing. If an applicant does not request a hearing within 15 days, the applicant waives its opportunity for hearing.
exceptions: Applicant may voluntarily request withdrawal under 21 CFR 314.150(c)

rules.rule_text: Approval of a drug under accelerated approval may be withdrawn if trials fail to verify clinical benefit or do not demonstrate sufficient clinical benefit to justify the risks, including showing a significantly smaller magnitude or duration of benefit than was anticipated based on the observed effect on the surrogate.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.3 — Withdrawal of Accelerated Approval
rules.verbatim_quote: Approval of a drug may be withdrawn if trials fail to verify clinical benefit or do not demonstrate sufficient clinical benefit to justify the risks associated with the drug (e.g., show a significantly smaller magnitude or duration of benefit than was anticipated based on the observed effect on the surrogate).

rules.rule_text: Priority review designation applies to an application for a drug that treats a serious condition and, if approved, would provide a significant improvement in safety or effectiveness. Priority review is determined at the time of NDA, BLA, or efficacy supplement filing. A priority designation means FDA's goal is to take action on the marketing application within 6 months of receipt (compared with 10 months under standard review).
rules.source_section: Section VIII — Priority Review Designation
rules.verbatim_quote: An application for a drug will receive priority review designation if it is for a drug that treats a serious condition and, if approved, would provide a significant improvement in safety or effectiveness. A priority review designation means FDA's goal is to take action on the marketing application within 6 months of receipt (compared with 10 months under standard review).

rules.rule_text: Significant improvement for priority review may be illustrated by: (1) evidence of increased effectiveness in treatment, prevention, or diagnosis of a condition; (2) elimination or substantial reduction of a treatment-limiting adverse reaction; (3) documented enhancement of patient compliance expected to lead to improvement in serious outcomes; or (4) evidence of safety and effectiveness in a new subpopulation. Comparison with available therapy in clinical testing is generally expected where available therapy exists.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.A.2 — Demonstrating the Potential To Be a Significant Improvement
rules.verbatim_quote: Significant improvement may be illustrated by the following examples: Evidence of increased effectiveness in treatment, prevention, or diagnosis of a condition; Elimination or substantial reduction of a treatment-limiting adverse reaction; Documented enhancement of patient compliance that is expected to lead to an improvement in serious outcomes; Evidence of safety and effectiveness in a new subpopulation.

rules.rule_text: For priority review with available therapy, sponsors should generally compare their investigational drug to the available therapy in clinical testing in an attempt to show superiority relating to either safety or effectiveness. Alternatively, sponsors could show the drug's ability to effectively treat patients unable to tolerate or unresponsive to available therapy, or that the drug can be used effectively with other critical agents that cannot be combined with available therapy.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.A.2 — Significant Improvement
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, if there is an available therapy, sponsors should compare their investigational drug to the available therapy in clinical testing with an attempt to show superiority relating to either safety or effectiveness. Alternatively, sponsors could show the drug's ability to effectively treat patients who are unable to tolerate, or whose disease failed to respond to, available therapy or show that the drug can be used effectively with other critical agents that cannot be combined with available therapy.
exceptions: Historical controls may also be persuasive per ICH E10

rules.rule_text: FDA determines priority review designation on a case-by-case basis at the time of NDA, BLA, or efficacy supplement filing. Priority review determination is not available for a designation at an earlier stage of development — unlike fast track and breakthrough therapy designations which can be obtained during development.
rules.source_section: Section VIII.A.2 — Priority Review
rules.verbatim_quote: On a case-by-case basis, FDA determines at the time of NDA, BLA, or efficacy supplement filing whether the proposed drug would be a significant improvement in the safety or effectiveness of the treatment, prevention, or diagnosis of a serious condition.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors receiving an expedited drug development designation (fast track or breakthrough therapy) may need to pursue a more rapid manufacturing development program. The sponsor's CMC teams should initiate early communication with FDA to ensure manufacturing development programs and timing of submissions meet agency expectations. Sponsors should be prepared to propose a commercial manufacturing program ensuring availability of quality product at the time of approval.
rules.source_section: Section IX.A — Manufacturing and Product Quality Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: The sponsor of a product that receives an expedited drug development designation may need to pursue a more rapid manufacturing development program to accommodate the accelerated pace of the clinical program. The sponsor's product quality and CMC teams should initiate early communication with FDA to ensure that the manufacturing development programs and timing of submissions meet the Agency's expectations for licensure or marketing approval.

rules.rule_text: For expedited program products, sponsors should allow for an earlier submission of the CMC section (including product quality information) for timely review, and critically, for inspection activities. Manufacturing facilities and equipment must be ready for inspection during review of the clinical section of the application. A comprehensive pre-submission meeting with FDA's product quality review groups is recommended. Discussions on proposed manufacturing plans should occur no later than the pre-NDA or pre-BLA meeting.
rules.source_section: Section IX.A — Manufacturing and Product Quality Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors of such products should allow for an earlier submission of the CMC section (including product quality information) for timely review, and, critically, for inspection activities. Coordination with the sponsor and contract manufacturers may be necessary to ensure that manufacturing facilities and equipment are ready for inspection during review of the clinical section of the application.
exceptions: FDA may exercise flexibility on manufacturing information for certain components (stability updates, validation, inspection planning, scale-up) on case-by-case basis

rules.rule_text: FDA may exercise flexibility on the type and extent of manufacturing information expected at the time of submission and approval for certain components for expedited program products. The level of flexibility is determined case-by-case based on: (1) product characteristics; (2) seriousness of the condition and medical need; (3) manufacturing processes; (4) robustness of the sponsor's quality system; and (5) strength of the sponsor's risk-based quality assessment.
rules.source_section: Section IX.A — Manufacturing and Product Quality Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Although sponsors must ensure the availability of quality product at the time of approval, FDA may exercise some flexibility on the type and extent of manufacturing information that is expected at the time of submission and approval for certain components (e.g., stability updates, validation strategies, inspection planning, manufacturing scale-up). The level of flexibility will be determined on a case-by-case basis after consideration of factors such as the following: (1) product characteristics, (2) seriousness of the condition and medical need, (3) manufacturing processes, (4) the robustness of the sponsor's quality system, and (5) the strength of the sponsor's risk-based quality assessment.

rules.rule_text: For expedited program products, sponsors should anticipate FDA's need to inspect clinical trials, including the analytical component of bioavailability or bioequivalence studies. Sponsors should be prepared for inspections to be scheduled early in the application review process so inspection results are available to inform the review division and to allow the sponsor to address significant inspection findings. Sponsors should initiate early communication with FDA about information required for inspection planning.
rules.source_section: Section IX.C — Clinical Inspection Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should anticipate the Agency's need to inspect clinical trials, including, if applicable, the analytical component of bioavailability or bioequivalence studies. Sponsors should be prepared for inspections to be scheduled by the Agency early in the application review process so inspection results are available to inform the review division and to allow time for the sponsor to address significant inspection findings.

rules.rule_text: Development programs using expedited program designations that involve an in vitro companion diagnostic device require consultation with FDA's companion diagnostics guidance. Sponsors should consult FDA's guidance on companion diagnostics prior to or during development.
rules.source_section: Section IX.D — Companion Diagnostics
rules.verbatim_quote: Development programs utilizing one or more of the expedited programs described in this guidance may involve an in vitro companion diagnostic device. Sponsors using one of the expedited programs for a product that involves an in vitro companion diagnostic device should consult FDA's guidance on the topic.

rules.rule_text: Fast track designation requests should be submitted to the IND administrative file to the attention of the appropriate review division or office in CDER or CBER. Requests may be submitted when the IND is first submitted or at any time thereafter before receiving marketing approval. FDA ordinarily should receive the request no later than the sponsor's pre-BLA or pre-NDA meeting, because many features of fast track designation will not apply after that time.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1.A.1-2 — When and Where to Send a Fast Track Designation Submission
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors may request fast track designation when the IND is first submitted or at any time thereafter before receiving marketing approval of their BLA or NDA. As a practical matter, FDA should ordinarily receive a fast track designation request no later than the sponsor's pre-BLA or pre-NDA meeting with the Agency because many of the features of fast track designation will not apply after that time.

rules.rule_text: Fast track designation request content requirements include: (1) cover letter identification as REQUEST FOR FAST TRACK DESIGNATION in bold, uppercase letters; (2) sponsor contact information in cover letter; (3) IND application number if applicable; (4) drug product proprietary name and active ingredient (or proper and proprietary name for biologics); (5) division/office where IND is active; (6) proposed indication(s); and (7) a concise summary supporting the designation request — including basis for serious condition determination and basis for potential to address unmet medical need.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1.A.3 — Content of a Fast Track Designation Submission
rules.verbatim_quote: Fast track designation requests should contain the following information (in most cases, this information could be captured in approximately 10 to 20 pages): If the fast track designation request is submitted to the sponsor's IND as an amendment, identification of the submission in the cover letter as a REQUEST FOR FAST TRACK DESIGNATION in bold, uppercase letters.

rules.rule_text: A fast track development program may lose its designation if: (1) the drug no longer demonstrates a potential to address unmet medical need; or (2) the drug is not being studied in a manner that shows it can treat a serious condition and meet an unmet medical need. Loss of fast track designation can occur, for example, if a new product is approved that addresses the same unmet need, or if emerging clinical data fails to show the anticipated advantage over available therapy.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1.A.5 — Continued Designation as a Fast Track Development Program
rules.verbatim_quote: A fast track development program may not continue to meet the criteria if the drug: (1) no longer demonstrates a potential to address unmet medical need or (2) is not being studied in a manner that shows the drug product can treat a serious condition and meets an unmet medical need.

rules.rule_text: Breakthrough therapy designation requests must contain the same administrative information as fast track requests, plus: the preliminary clinical evidence that the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over available therapies. This includes: a brief description of available therapies and their effectiveness; justification for the comparator selected; the study design, population, and endpoint used; and a brief description of study results and statistical analyses including subgroup analyses. FDA does not expect the sponsor to submit primary data sets.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1.B.3 — Content of a Breakthrough Therapy Designation Submission
rules.verbatim_quote: A concise summary of information that supports the breakthrough therapy designation request for the indication being studied, including the following: The preliminary clinical evidence that the drug may demonstrate substantial improvement over available therapies. FDA does not expect the sponsor to submit primary data (data sets); but, the sponsor should describe the preliminary clinical evidence, including, for example, a brief description of available therapies (if there are any) and their effectiveness; justification for the comparator selected for the clinical studies, the study design, the population studied, and the endpoint used; and a brief description of the study results and statistical analyses (including, for example, subgroup analysis).

rules.rule_text: A breakthrough therapy development program may lose its designation if, over the course of development, the drug no longer meets the criteria. For example, if subsequent interim data from a larger study shows a response substantially smaller than the response seen in early clinical testing that initially supported the designation.
rules.source_section: Appendix 1.B.5 — Continued Designation as a Breakthrough Therapy Development Program
rules.verbatim_quote: Over the course of drug development, it can be expected that some products granted breakthrough therapy designation will no longer be considered a breakthrough therapy. For example, a drug development program may be granted breakthrough therapy designation using early clinical testing that shows a much higher response rate than available therapies. However, subsequent interim data derived from a larger study may show a response that is substantially smaller than the response seen in early clinical testing.

rules.rule_text: The four expedited programs available for drugs for serious conditions are: (1) Fast Track designation — during IND, for serious condition + unmet need potential; (2) Breakthrough Therapy designation — during IND, for serious condition + preliminary clinical evidence of substantial improvement; (3) Accelerated Approval — at NDA/BLA, for serious condition + meaningful advantage + surrogate/intermediate endpoint; and (4) Priority Review designation — at NDA/BLA filing, for serious condition + significant improvement in safety or effectiveness. These programs are not mutually exclusive.
rules.source_section: Section IV — Overview of Expedited Programs
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA has established four programs to expedite the development and review of drugs to treat serious or life-threatening conditions: fast track designation, breakthrough therapy designation, accelerated approval, and priority review designation.
exceptions: Programs are not mutually exclusive; a drug may receive multiple designations

rules.rule_text: When the same clinical trial is used to support accelerated approval and to verify clinical benefit for traditional approval (i.e., the trial demonstrates both surrogate-endpoint and clinical-endpoint effects), the protocol and statistical analysis plan must clearly account for analyses of both the surrogate endpoint data (for accelerated approval) and the continuation of randomized trial(s) to obtain clinical endpoint data (for verification of clinical benefit). When FDA verifies clinical benefit via the same trial, the data to verify clinical benefit may be nearly complete by the time of accelerated approval.
rules.source_section: Section VII.D.2 — Confirmatory Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: When it is possible to use a later effect in a trial to verify the effect seen earlier in the same trial that supported accelerated approval, the same clinical trial(s) can be used to support accelerated approval and verify and describe the clinical benefit. In this case, the protocol and the statistical analysis plan should clearly account for an analysis of the surrogate endpoint data to provide support for accelerated approval, with continuation of the randomized trial(s) to obtain data on the clinical endpoint that will be the basis for verifying the clinical benefit.
